title: if and else
instruction:
Standard if/else — no surprises.

Run this.
successMessage: C# requires parentheses around the condition (unlike Go or Rust). Braces are required for the body.

title: else if chains
instruction:
Chain multiple conditions.

Run this, then change score to 75 and 55.
successMessage: The first true condition wins. Braces are optional for single-statement bodies — but many teams require them for clarity.

title: Ternary operator
instruction:
The ternary `? :` is a compact if/else expression:

```csharp
string result = x > 0 ? "positive" : "non-positive";
```

Run this.
successMessage: Ternary is clean for simple two-way choices. Avoid nesting ternaries — they become unreadable quickly.

title: switch statement
instruction:
Match a single value against multiple cases.

Run this.
successMessage: break is required after each case (no fall-through by default). default catches everything else.

title: switch expression (modern C#)
instruction:
C# 8+ switch expression is terser and returns a value.

Run this.
successMessage: Switch expressions produce a value. _ is the discard/default. No break needed. Much cleaner than the classic switch statement.

title: Logical operators
instruction:
&&, ||, ! are short-circuit evaluated — right side is not evaluated if left side determines the result.

Run this.
successMessage: Short-circuit: if age >= 18 is false, hasId is never evaluated. Useful for null checks: obj != null && obj.Value > 0.

title: Null conditional and coalescing
instruction:
C# has operators for null safety:
- `??` returns a default if null
- `?.` calls a method only if not null

Run this.
successMessage: ?? returns the right side when the left is null. ?. avoids NullReferenceException by returning null instead of throwing when the object is null.

title: Pattern matching in if
instruction:
C# 7+ `is` patterns match types and bind variables.

Run this.
successMessage: 'is int n' both checks the type AND binds the value to n. It is type-safe pattern matching — no explicit cast needed.
